comments.author: @NICEGLOBE05
comments.text: Beyond the turquoise waters and luxury resorts, what touches me most is the historical depth of the island. From Le Morne Brabant to Ganga Talao, Mauritius carries a poignant memory of freedom and identity. The warmth and resilience of the Mauritian people are what make this island truly paradise. Excellent 4K visuals!

comments.author: @RemoteEarthJourneys
comments.text: *Mauritius might be one of the few places on Earth where history, culture, and nature somehow fit together on one tiny island. What surprised you the most: the underwater waterfall, the story of Le Morne, or the fact that four continents live side by side?*

comments.author: @michellemaistry3170
comments.text: I've been to Mauritius in 1990 and it is indeed a truly beautiful place. I was in awe even at that young an age.  We lived in Coin De Mere on the north coast of the island and we toured the tourist destinations only .... although I wish I could go again as an adult this time, and explore like a local, all the hidden gem places. Yes, the people are super friendly and I was learning French from the Bible at our self catering resort. I went to the 7 coloured sands site and the camera footage on this video is rather dim compared to its natural vibrancy. Port Louis was also tranquil, and the nude beach was sO serene with white sand for miles on that island. I fell in love with isle Maurice ~•❤•~

comments.author: @wesdale1753
comments.text:
Thank you for this magnificent presentation of the Dodo island. Named after the Dutch Prince Maurice Van Nassau.
After the Dutch, the Portuguese were here,then the French and the British.
On a clear night, lights from nearby Reunion island can be seen from Le Morne mount. Further south after the tea plantation, a couple of Pyramid stone structures can be seen. No one knows who build them. Canada / 🇲🇺 Mauritius.

comments.author: @SlegsBlankes
comments.text: Belgium here, 3 pm. Been countless times to Mauritius back in the days, mostly business, many holidays too. Fantastic staff in fantastic hotels. Fond memories, lovely people, great food, swimming, snorkelling, sailing, still have two Chazal (well known local artist) watercolours hanging in my living room, one depicting the extinct Dodo. I should go back one last time…

comments.author: @nettypical9960
comments.text: Those seven-colored sand dunes that never mix together no matter how much you shake them in a bottle? It sounds unbelievable! I need to see Chamarel with my own eyes.

replies.author: @RemoteEarthJourneys
replies.text: I think that's exactly why Chamarel fascinates so many people. It's one of those rare places that sounds like a myth until you realize it's actually real. What's even more amazing is that millions of years of volcanic history created those colors—and somehow nature still keeps them perfectly separated. Which color do you think you'd be most excited to see in person?

comments.author: @Aventurex-686
comments.text: Mauritius reminds me that the world doesn't always need bigger borders; sometimes it just needs bigger dinner tables. One island welcomes influences from four continents without losing its own identity. Meanwhile, I can't even keep my backyard barbecue organized. What surprised you most about island life?
